Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent routine, honestly. Being 29 and juggling multiple responsibilities, I had to carve out specific blocks of time for studying—usually early mornings before work and late evenings. I'd dedicate about 24 hours a week across my 16 online courses, which sounds like a lot but it's spread thin. I relied heavily on audio materials since I learn best by listening, so I'd often have lectures playing while doing other tasks around the house.
Q2: I aimed for regularity because that's the only way I could manage everything. Tuesdays and Thursdays were my heavy study days, and weekends I'd catch up on anything I missed. But honestly, with 16 courses running simultaneously, sometimes deadlines would pile up and throw my whole schedule off. When that happened, I'd have to prioritize whichever assignment was due soonest, which meant some courses got neglected temporarily.
Q3: Usually it was a combination of habit and necessity. I'd log in first thing in the morning to check announcements and see if any new materials were posted. Sometimes I'd get email notifications about discussion posts or upcoming deadlines that would prompt me to jump in. The mindfulness exercises I started doing actually helped me be more intentional about logging in rather than just reacting to stress.
Q4: Yes, definitely. After the first few weeks, logging in became almost automatic—like checking email. I set a reminder on my phone for 7 AM to open the LMS, and that consistency really helped. The discussion forums especially became a regular thing because I enjoyed connecting with other students. It took about three weeks before it felt natural rather than forced.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me—I watched over 150 minutes worth because that's how I absorb information best. I'd often replay sections multiple times, letting the audio sink in. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing other perspectives helped concepts click. Peer interactions gave me different angles on the material that I wouldn't have discovered studying alone.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete as many assignments as I should have—only about 31% of them. It wasn't that I didn't value them, but with limited resources and time spread across so many courses, I had to make tough choices. Reading-heavy PDFs often got pushed aside because I struggle to focus on text for long periods. I'd skim them but rarely engaged deeply unless absolutely necessary.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to the bigger picture or to the exam. Audio and video content always felt worthwhile because that's my strength. Discussion posts felt valuable when the conversation was active and people were genuinely engaging, not just posting to meet requirements. If something felt like busywork with no clear learning outcome, I'd deprioritize it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With my workload and limited resources, required activities had to come first. But sometimes the optional ones were actually more interesting—like supplementary podcasts or extra discussion threads. When I did engage with optional content, it felt more relaxed and enjoyable because there was no pressure attached to it.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge portion of my studying happened offline. I'd download audio lectures and listen to them during my commute or while cooking. I took handwritten notes that I'd review repeatedly—something the LMS would never capture. I also used free YouTube videos and podcasts on similar topics when the course materials weren't clicking for me. My notebook is filled with mind maps and summaries that represent hours of work.
Q10: There were many times I'd be actively studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd download a lecture once and listen to it five or six times over the week. I'd discuss concepts with a study partner over the phone—we'd talk through problems for hours. I also spent significant time just thinking through concepts while doing mindfulness exercises, which actually helped me process and retain information better.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a video lecture once, download the audio, and then study it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable given my limited resources, so downloading materials was essential. I'd also screenshot important slides and review them on my phone during breaks at work. The LMS would show one access, but that material got used extensively offline.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. The stress was overwhelming—I'd rate it quite high—and even though I tried to stay motivated, my resilience isn't great. There were weeks when personal responsibilities piled up alongside academic demands, and I just couldn't give the courses the attention they deserved. The mindfulness intervention actually helped me get through those rough patches without completely falling apart.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a constant companion—working, studying 24 hours a week, managing life on limited resources. When I was exhausted, I'd still log into the LMS but my engagement was shallow. I'd watch videos but not really absorb them. Confusion in certain courses made me avoid those materials entirely sometimes because I didn't have the mental energy to push through the difficulty.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere—worried about bills or just mentally drained. I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't retained anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning. It was frustrating because I wanted to engage but couldn't always muster the focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments frequently, which is why my completion rate was so low. It wasn't laziness—I knew they were important. But when you're stretched thin across 16 courses with limited resources, you have to triage. I'd tell myself I'd get to an assignment 'tomorrow' and then tomorrow would bring new urgent tasks. The assignments I did complete, I scored well on, which made the incomplete ones even more frustrating.
Q16: Deadlines were both motivating and stressful. They'd force me to prioritize and actually complete things, but the pressure sometimes felt paralyzing. I'd often do a burst of activity right before deadlines—logging in more frequently, watching videos I'd been putting off. Without deadlines, I think I'd have engaged even less consistently. They were the external structure I needed to keep moving forward.
Q17: When I received feedback, especially positive feedback on the assignments I did complete, it boosted my motivation significantly. Seeing that I scored well—like 87% on assignments—reminded me that I could do this despite all my constraints. Constructive criticism helped me adjust my approach. But sometimes feedback came too late to be useful, or I was already overwhelmed with new tasks to really incorporate it.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and challenging. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to work within. But with 16 courses, the structures sometimes conflicted—deadlines would cluster together impossibly. I appreciated courses that released materials early so I could work ahead during lighter weeks. The discussion requirements kept me socially connected to the course even when I was struggling.
Q19: Courses with strong audio components were much easier for me to engage with—that matched my learning style perfectly. Flexible deadlines or multiple submission attempts would have helped enormously. What made engagement harder was when courses were text-heavy with no audio alternatives, or when the workload assumptions didn't account for students managing multiple courses. The peer interaction features were a bright spot though.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat—high activity but lower assignment completion. For me, it was about engagement versus output. I was genuinely learning and participating in discussions and watching videos, but translating that into submitted work required additional time and energy I didn't always have. Sometimes understanding the material and producing formal assignments are two different challenges, especially with limited resources.
Q21: That makes sense to me too. Someone might be studying extensively offline—like I did with downloaded materials—and performing well on exams without showing much LMS activity. My exam score was decent even though my attendance was low. The LMS captures platform use, not actual learning. A student could be deeply engaged through textbooks, study groups, or other resources that never touch the LMS.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, time spent on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't capture whether I was actually absorbing a video or just had it playing. They miss all offline study, all the mental processing that happens away from the screen. They show behavior, not learning. They're a partial picture at best.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and connect with the material. It's not just about logging in or watching videos—it's about whether my brain is working on the concepts. For me, engagement often looks like listening to a lecture multiple times, discussing ideas with peers, and finding myself thinking about course concepts during my daily life. It's a state of mind, not a metric.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the mindfulness intervention actually helped me manage my stress and stay in the course when I wanted to give up. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials or the phone calls with study partners. It can't measure how motivated I stayed despite high stress and limited resources, or the genuine surprise and satisfaction I felt when I passed. My journey was much richer than any log could show.
